stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 19-year-old woman with severe anorexia nervosa (BMI 13.4, weight 36 kg, recent 5 kg loss over 4 weeks) to a general medical ward. She has been eating less than 500 kcal per day for 2 months and reports occasional dizzy spells. She is alert, hydrated and apyrexial. Bloods on arrival show low normal electrolytes and the dietitian has commenced a structured refeeding plan starting at 20 kcal/kg/day.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.2, HR 48 sinus, BP 88/52, RR 14, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Cachectic; lanugo hair; dry skin; reduced subcutaneous fat; alert, no focal deficits
data_table.Investigations: Na 137, K 3.4, urea 4.2, Cr 76, phosphate 0.74, magnesium 0.68, glucose 4.0, ALT 22, ECG QTc 470 ms

lead_in: Which prophylactic regimen is most appropriate before commencing the refeeding plan?

options.text: Oral thiamine 300 mg daily, oral phosphate 500 mg three times daily, and daily phosphate, magnesium and potassium monitoring for 7 days
options.rationale: Severe malnutrition with BMI under 14 and 2 months of low intake meets very high refeeding-risk criteria; prophylactic thiamine, phosphate replacement and daily electrolyte monitoring for 7 to 10 days are standard. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous insulin 0.05 unit/kg/hour to maintain glucose between 4 and 6 mmol/L during refeeding; routine vitamin replacement
options.rationale: Insulin is not used in refeeding; insulin surge from carbohydrate reintroduction is the underlying mechanism of refeeding syndrome and pharmacological insulin worsens this. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Rapid escalation to 40 kcal/kg/day from day 2 with weekly biochemistry monitoring; restrict thiamine to symptomatic patients
options.rationale: Rapid escalation in severe malnutrition causes refeeding syndrome (severe hypophosphataemia, cardiac arrhythmia, Wernicke); slow escalation with daily monitoring is standard. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold nutrition for 48 hours; correct electrolytes intravenously first before any oral intake; defer thiamine
options.rationale: Withholding nutrition prolongs starvation; refeeding is started cautiously with electrolyte replacement and thiamine before the first calories. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Parenteral nutrition only for 5 days; resume enteral feeding at 30 kcal/kg/day once electrolytes have normalised
options.rationale: Parenteral nutrition increases line infection and metabolic complication risk; enteral feeding is preferred when the gut is functional, even in severe malnutrition. (Source: ANZAED/RANZCP Eating Disorders Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome is precipitated by carbohydrate reintroduction in severe malnutrition; insulin surge drives intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium and magnesium causing severe hypophosphataemia, cardiac arrhythmia, cardiac failure and Wernicke encephalopathy. Australian guidance for high-risk eating disorder admissions starts at 20 kcal/kg/day, escalates over 7 to 10 days, prescribes prophylactic thiamine 300 mg daily, phosphate replacement, and daily monitoring of phosphate, magnesium, potassium and ECG. ECG monitoring is essential as QTc prolongation and arrhythmia are leading causes of death.
key_points: Start refeeding at 20 kcal/kg/day in high-risk patients
Prophylactic thiamine 300 mg daily and phosphate replacement
Daily electrolytes and ECG for 7 to 10 days
Avoid parenteral nutrition when gut functional
explanation.pearls: Phosphate below 0.6 mmol/L during refeeding warrants IV phosphate replacement (e.g. 30 mmol over 12 hours) and slowing calorie escalation.

stem: You are the emergency medicine registrar managing a 26-year-old woman 90 minutes after deliberate ingestion of 60 amitriptyline 50 mg tablets (3 g total). She is drowsy with GCS 9 (E2V3M4). The team has secured the airway with a nasopharyngeal adjunct and applied oxygen. Vitals and bloods are back. A first 12-lead ECG shows broad complexes with QRS 140 ms and a tall terminal R wave in aVR.

data_table.Vital signs: GCS 9, BP 88/52, HR 128 sinus, RR 22, SpO2 96% on 6 L mask, T 36.4
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, mydriasis 6 mm reactive, dry skin, urinary retention; absent bowel sounds; lateral chest clear
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.30, lactate 3.0, K 3.6; ECG QRS 140 ms, terminal R in aVR 4 mm, QTc 510 ms; paracetamol negative

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous lipid emulsion 20 percent 1.5 mL/kg bolus over 1 minute; defer sodium bicarbonate until ECG worsens
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is reserved for cardiovascular collapse refractory to standard measures including sodium bicarbonate; first-line for TCA-induced QRS widening is bicarbonate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus and prepare for intubation; recheck ECG and pH 5 minutes after bolus
options.rationale: QRS above 100 ms or terminal R in aVR above 3 mm in TCA overdose indicates sodium-channel blockade with high risk of seizures and arrhythmia; IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg with airway protection is first line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg every minute to a maximum of 1 mg; arrange psychiatric review for risk assessment
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it can precipitate seizures; benzodiazepines are not the toxin here. (Source: AMH: Flumazenil)

options.text: Synchronised DC cardioversion at 100 J for the broad-complex tachycardia; followed by lignocaine infusion 1 mg/kg loading
options.rationale: Sinus tachycardia with QRS widening from sodium channel blockade does not require cardioversion; class Ic and Ia antiarrhythmics worsen sodium channel blockade. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube; defer airway intervention and observe for 4 hours before further escalation
options.rationale: Charcoal in a patient with GCS 9 without secured airway risks aspiration; airway protection precedes decontamination, and bicarbonate is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose causes sodium-channel blockade with QRS widening, terminal R wave in aVR, hypotension and seizure risk. QRS above 100 ms or terminal R in aVR above 3 mm predicts arrhythmia and seizures. First-line treatment is IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg, repeated to maintain QRS below 100 ms and arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55, alongside airway protection and benzodiazepines for seizures. Lipid emulsion is reserved for refractory cardiovascular collapse. Hypertonic saline is an alternative source of sodium load when bicarbonate excess limits use.
key_points: Terminal R in aVR above 3 mm or QRS above 100 ms = sodium channel toxicity
Bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV first line
Avoid class Ia and Ic antiarrhythmics and flumazenil
Lipid emulsion for refractory collapse
explanation.pearls: Aim arterial pH between 7.45 and 7.55 with serial bicarbonate boluses; sodium load and alkalinisation both reduce sodium channel blockade.

stem: You are the medical registrar in ED reviewing a 68-year-old man with 24 hours of fever, dysuria and confusion. He has a background of well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin. He is hypotensive on arrival. The team has placed two large bore IV lines and a urinary catheter for output monitoring. Bloods are at the lab.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.8, HR 122, BP 84/52, RR 26, SpO2 96% on 6 L mask, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable; warm peripheries; suprapubic tenderness; cloudy malodorous urine; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2, WCC 18.2 (neutrophils 16.2), CRP 240, Cr 168 (baseline 95), urine dip nitrite and leukocyte positive, blood cultures pending
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD; perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous noradrenaline infusion via peripheral line at 0.05 mcg/kg/min; defer fluid resuscitation pending central access
options.rationale: Initial fluid resuscitation precedes vasopressors in sepsis; vasopressors are added if hypotension persists after 30 mL/kg crystalloid. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Oral co-trimoxazole 160/800 mg twice daily and oral fluids; admit to short-stay for 24 hours observation with repeat lactate
options.rationale: Septic shock with lactate above 4 requires IV antibiotics and resuscitation; oral therapy is not appropriate for an unstable patient. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Crystalloid 30 mL/kg IV over 3 hours; IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g; blood cultures and lactate before antibiotics
options.rationale: Sepsis hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, take cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotic within 1 hour, crystalloid 30 mL/kg over 3 hours, vasopressors if MAP below 65 after fluid. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Activated protein C infusion 24 mcg/kg/hour for 96 hours; broad-spectrum antibiotics after coagulopathy is excluded
options.rationale: Activated protein C was withdrawn from the market after PROWESS-SHOCK showed no benefit; it is not part of current sepsis management. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg as first-line therapy; defer antibiotics until cortisol response confirmed
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone 200 mg per day is added in fluid-refractory and vasopressor-dependent septic shock, not as first-line ahead of antibiotics. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle is: measure lactate, take blood cultures before antibiotics, give broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, crystalloid 30 mL/kg over 3 hours if hypotensive or lactate above 4, and add vasopressors if MAP remains below 65 despite fluid. Hydrocortisone 200 mg daily is added in fluid-refractory shock. Source control identifies the focus and removes infected hardware or pus. Reassess perfusion using lactate clearance, capillary refill and dynamic markers.
key_points: Cultures before antibiotics; antibiotics within 1 hour
Crystalloid 30 mL/kg over 3 hours for hypotension or lactate above 4
Vasopressors if MAP below 65 after fluids
Hydrocortisone in fluid-refractory shock
explanation.pearls: Empirical antibiotic for urosepsis in Australia is piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 8 hours, or ceftriaxone 2 g if community-acquired without risk factors for resistant organisms.

subtopic_detail: Necrotising fasciitis recognition and LRINEC score
stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 58-year-old man with 18 hours of right thigh swelling and pain disproportionate to the redness. He has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2 percent). The skin is dusky over a 12 by 8 cm area with bullae forming and crepitus on palpation. He is febrile and unwell. Vitals and bloods are back.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.0, HR 124, BP 96/58, RR 24, SpO2 96% on 4 L mask
data_table.Examination: Pain out of proportion; bullae and dusky discolouration on right thigh; crepitus palpable; reduced sensation over the central plaque
data_table.Investigations: Hb 138, WCC 22, Na 128, glucose 18.4, Cr 132, CRP 380, lactate 4.0
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD; gliclazide MR 60 mg daily; insulin glargine 18 units nocte

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Outline cellulitis with marker pen; commence IV cefazolin 2 g and observe for response over 4 hours
options.rationale: Pain disproportionate to skin findings, bullae, crepitus and systemic features suggest necrotising fasciitis; cellulitis monotherapy delays critical surgical intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Bedside ultrasound to exclude deep abscess; commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for outpatient management
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency, not a candidate for ultrasound and outpatient oral antibiotics; rapid spread without prompt surgery is fatal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy after first dose of antibiotics; defer surgery pending response to medical therapy and hyperbaric
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen is adjunctive at most and never substitutes for source control; urgent surgical debridement is the definitive treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent surgical consult for debridement; IV meropenem 1 g, vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading, clindamycin 600 mg; activate sepsis pathway
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis features (pain out of proportion, bullae, crepitus, hypotension, LRINEC 8) mandate urgent surgical debridement plus empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics including clindamycin for anti-toxin effect. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the thigh to delineate fascial involvement; defer antibiotics until imaging completed and reviewed
options.rationale: MRI delays definitive surgery in suspected necrotising fasciitis; clinical diagnosis with prompt surgery and antibiotics is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency suggested by pain out of proportion to skin findings, dusky discolouration, bullae, crepitus, anaesthesia over the lesion, rapid spread and systemic toxicity. The LRINEC score (CRP, WCC, Hb, Na, Cr, glucose) supports the diagnosis; score 8 or above indicates high risk but should never delay surgical consultation. Management is urgent surgical debridement, broad-spectrum antibiotics (carbapenem, vancomycin, clindamycin for anti-toxin effect), source control and intensive care.
key_points: Necrotising fasciitis features: pain out of proportion, bullae, crepitus
LRINEC 8 or above is high risk but not required for surgery
Urgent debridement is the principal intervention
Empirical antibiotics: carbapenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin
explanation.pearls: Mortality in necrotising fasciitis approaches 30 to 50 percent and rises with each hour of delay to surgical debridement; never delay surgery for imaging.

subtopic_detail: Latent TB diagnosis and treatment selection
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 34-year-old asymptomatic nurse before commencing infliximab for rheumatoid arthritis. She was born in Australia and has no known TB contact. She did NOT have BCG as a child. She has a routine pre-biologic screen including chest x-ray and QuantiFERON Gold Plus.

data_table.Examination: No respiratory symptoms; normal chest examination; no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Chest x-ray normal; QuantiFERON Gold Plus positive (TB1 1.2 IU/mL, TB2 1.4 IU/mL); HIV negative; HBV and HCV negative; FBC, U&E, LFTs normal
data_table.Medications: Methotrexate 15 mg weekly; folic acid 5 mg weekly

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure and proceed with infliximab; treat only if active TB develops during therapy on routine monitoring
options.rationale: Positive interferon gamma release assay before TNF inhibitor warrants treatment for latent TB; missed treatment carries up to 10 percent reactivation risk on biologic. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Repeat QuantiFERON in 3 months to confirm; proceed with infliximab in the interim while monitoring symptoms
options.rationale: Confirmed positive QuantiFERON does not require repeat for confirmation; biologic should not be started without treating latent TB. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence 6-month course of pyrazinamide and ethambutol with monthly LFTs and visual acuity assessment
options.rationale: Pyrazinamide plus ethambutol is part of treatment for active TB, not latent infection; latent TB is treated with isoniazid, rifampicin or short-course combinations. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer to public health for directly observed therapy with daily isoniazid for 24 months as an immunosuppressed patient
options.rationale: Standard latent TB regimens are 6 to 9 months of isoniazid or 4 months of rifampicin; 24-month therapy is not a current standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence isoniazid 5 mg/kg daily plus pyridoxine 25 mg daily for 9 months; defer infliximab until at least 4 weeks of therapy
options.rationale: Positive QuantiFERON before TNF inhibitor mandates latent TB treatment; isoniazid 9 months (or rifampicin 4 months) is first line; pyridoxine prevents neuropathy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Latent TB infection screening with an interferon gamma release assay (QuantiFERON Gold Plus or T-SPOT.TB) is standard before TNF inhibitor or organ transplant. Tuberculin skin testing is an alternative where IGRA is unavailable. Confirmed latent TB is treated with isoniazid for 9 months plus pyridoxine, isoniazid plus rifapentine weekly for 3 months (3HP), or rifampicin alone for 4 months. Biologic therapy is deferred until at least 4 weeks of treatment. Active disease must be excluded before treatment of latent infection.
key_points: IGRA preferred before TNF inhibitor or transplant
Treat latent TB with isoniazid 9 months plus pyridoxine, or 3HP, or rifampicin 4 months
Defer biologic by 4 weeks after starting LTBI treatment
Always exclude active TB before treating latent infection
explanation.pearls: Pyridoxine 25 mg daily is added with isoniazid in patients at risk of neuropathy (diabetes, alcohol use, pregnancy, HIV, malnutrition) to prevent peripheral neuropathy.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 68-year-old postmenopausal woman 6 months after a low-trauma wrist fracture. She is otherwise well, not on glucocorticoids, and her father had a hip fracture aged 78. She has had a DXA scan today. Her calcium and vitamin D are replete and her eGFR is 76 mL/min/1.73m^2. She is keen to consider preventive therapy.

data_table.Examination: No spinal tenderness; height 162 cm (lost 2 cm); BMI 24
data_table.Investigations: DXA T-score lumbar spine -2.6, femoral neck -2.4; corrected calcium 2.40, 25-OH vitamin D 78, PTH 4.2, ALP 80, Cr 76, FRAX 10-year major fracture 22%, hip fracture 6%
data_table.Medications: Vitamin D 1000 IU daily; calcium 600 mg daily; nil other

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological choice?

options.text: Alendronate 70 mg oral once weekly; continue calcium and vitamin D; reassess BMD and clinical risk at 5 years
options.rationale: Prior low-trauma fracture and T-score below -2.5 meet PBS criteria for oral bisphosphonate; alendronate weekly is first line; review at 5 years considers drug holiday. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneous 6-monthly indefinitely; cease calcium supplementation to avoid hypercalcaemia
options.rationale: Denosumab is second line where bisphosphonate is poorly tolerated or contraindicated; ceasing calcium is unsafe and risks hypocalcaemia with denosumab. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Hormone replacement therapy with oral oestradiol and progesterone; bone density monitoring annually for 5 years
options.rationale: HRT is not first-line for established osteoporosis in a 68-year-old; absolute thromboembolism risk exceeds vasomotor benefit at this age. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Teriparatide 20 mcg subcutaneous daily for 24 months as first-line agent; defer bisphosphonate during teriparatide
options.rationale: Teriparatide is reserved for severe osteoporosis with multiple vertebral fractures or failure of bisphosphonate, not first-line. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Raloxifene 60 mg daily; reassess BMD at 5 years and consider switch if no response on vertebral measurement
options.rationale: Raloxifene is reserved for vertebral fracture prevention in women intolerant of bisphosphonates; it does not reduce hip fracture risk and is not first line here. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

explanation.summary: Australian PBS criteria for antiresorptive therapy include prior minimal trauma fracture, T-score -2.5 or below, or high fracture risk by FRAX. First-line is oral alendronate 70 mg weekly or risedronate 35 mg weekly, alongside calcium and vitamin D. Intravenous zoledronic acid 5 mg annually is an alternative when oral therapy is poorly tolerated. Denosumab is second line; teriparatide is reserved for severe disease. Drug holiday is considered at 5 years for bisphosphonates; denosumab cannot be stopped without follow-on therapy due to rebound vertebral fracture risk.
key_points: Prior minimal-trauma fracture or T-score below -2.5 meets PBS criteria
Oral alendronate or risedronate first line; IV zoledronate alternative
Denosumab second line; never stop without follow-on therapy
Bisphosphonate drug holiday considered at 5 years
explanation.pearls: Denosumab cessation without follow-on bisphosphonate causes multiple vertebral fracture rebound within 9 to 18 months due to rapid loss of antiresorptive effect.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 52-year-old man on day 3 of treatment for native aortic valve endocarditis with methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. He has new dyspnoea overnight. Echocardiogram now shows a 1.2 cm aortic vegetation, moderate to severe aortic regurgitation and a small pericardial effusion. He has bilateral fine inspiratory crackles. He has had 3 days of IV flucloxacillin and remains febrile to 38.7.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, BP 102/56, HR 108, RR 26, SpO2 92% on 4 L mask, JVP 5 cm
data_table.Examination: Bibasal fine crackles; soft S3; collapsing pulse; soft diastolic murmur at LSE; warm extremities
data_table.Investigations: Hb 110, WCC 14, Cr 138 (baseline 92), CRP 180, troponin 84, BNP 1200; echo aortic vegetation 12 mm, severe AR, EF 50%, no abscess yet; chest x-ray pulmonary oedema

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue IV flucloxacillin for a further 7 days; reassess vegetation size and clinical response with echo on day 10
options.rationale: Heart failure from severe acute aortic regurgitation in infective endocarditis is a class I indication for urgent surgery; delay risks decompensation and death. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Endocarditis Position Statement)

options.text: Urgent cardiothoracic referral for aortic valve replacement within 24 to 48 hours; continue antibiotics throughout
options.rationale: New heart failure from severe valvular regurgitation in active endocarditis is a class I indication for urgent surgery within 24 to 48 hours; ongoing antibiotics continue around surgery. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Endocarditis Position Statement)

options.text: Add IV gentamicin 1 mg/kg every 8 hours for synergy; reassess response after 5 days before considering operative referral
options.rationale: Routine gentamicin in MSSA native valve endocarditis is no longer recommended due to nephrotoxicity without survival benefit; surgery is indicated here irrespective. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Endocarditis Position Statement)

options.text: Switch to IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose; defer surgery pending repeat MRI brain to exclude embolic stroke
options.rationale: MSSA is best treated with flucloxacillin; vancomycin is inferior in MSSA. Embolic risk does not generally delay urgent surgery for haemodynamic indications. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Endocarditis Position Statement)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg with non-invasive ventilation for pulmonary oedema; cardiology review in 1 week for assessment
options.rationale: Medical therapy temporises but does not address acute severe AR; delay for 1 week risks fatal decompensation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Endocarditis Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Indications for early surgery in active infective endocarditis include heart failure from valve dysfunction, uncontrolled infection (persistent bacteraemia despite appropriate antibiotics, perivalvular extension, abscess, fistula), and prevention of embolism (mobile vegetations greater than 10 mm with prior embolism or larger than 15 mm). Heart failure due to severe valvular regurgitation is the most common class I indication and typically requires surgery within 24 to 48 hours. Antibiotics are continued throughout the perioperative period and adjusted based on intra-operative cultures.
key_points: Heart failure from valve dysfunction is class I urgent surgical indication
Uncontrolled infection (abscess, persistent bacteraemia) also class I
Vegetations greater than 10 to 15 mm with embolic risk warrant surgery
Antibiotics continue around surgery
explanation.pearls: MSSA native valve endocarditis is treated with flucloxacillin 2 g every 4 hours for 4 to 6 weeks; vancomycin is reserved for MRSA or severe penicillin allergy.

stem: You are the medical registrar in an outpatient clinic reviewing a 38-year-old woman with 18 months of weight gain, easy bruising, proximal muscle weakness when climbing stairs, and new-onset hypertension. Her BMI is 34 and she has wide violaceous abdominal striae. She also reports irregular menses. She is not on glucocorticoids by any route. Her sleep is good and her depression screen is negative.

data_table.Examination: Central obesity; thin extremities; proximal weakness on hip flexion; violaceous striae greater than 1 cm; supraclavicular fat pad
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 6.8 fasting, HbA1c 6.2%, K 3.4, ACTH pending; menstrual irregularity 2 to 3 months; no glucocorticoid use
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily; no inhaled corticosteroid

lead_in: Which screening test sequence is most appropriate?

options.text: Random morning serum cortisol and plasma ACTH; CT adrenals only if cortisol greater than 700 nmol/L
options.rationale: Random cortisol does not screen for Cushing's because of pulsatility; loss of diurnal variation is the key abnormality, requiring late-night cortisol or 1 mg DST. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Guideline)

options.text: Adrenal CT alone; biochemical testing only if a mass greater than 1 cm is identified on imaging
options.rationale: Imaging is not a screening test for Cushing's syndrome; biochemical confirmation of hypercortisolism precedes localisation imaging. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Guideline)

options.text: Two of: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test, late-night salivary cortisol, or 24-hour urinary free cortisol
options.rationale: Endocrine Society guidance recommends two abnormal first-line tests from 1 mg overnight DST, late-night salivary cortisol or 24-hour urinary free cortisol to confirm Cushing's syndrome before localisation. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Guideline)

options.text: Insulin tolerance test for cortisol response; pituitary MRI only if cortisol fails to rise above 500 nmol/L during test
options.rationale: Insulin tolerance test assesses adrenal insufficiency, not Cushing's; it is not a Cushing's screen. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Guideline)

options.text: Serum cortisol after 2 mg dexamethasone 8 mg over 2 days; pituitary MRI as first-line localisation if cortisol suppresses
options.rationale: High-dose dexamethasone suppression (8 mg) is used to differentiate Cushing's disease from ectopic ACTH after confirmation; it is not first-line screening. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Guideline)

explanation.summary: Cushing's syndrome screening uses first-line tests that capture loss of diurnal cortisol rhythm or feedback suppression: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol below 50 nmol/L excludes), late-night salivary cortisol (low at night in normal), and 24-hour urinary free cortisol. Two abnormal first-line tests confirm hypercortisolism. Subsequent ACTH measurement classifies as ACTH-dependent (pituitary adenoma or ectopic) vs ACTH-independent (adrenal). Localisation by MRI pituitary or adrenal CT follows.
key_points: Screen with 1 mg DST, late-night salivary cortisol, or 24-hour UFC
Two abnormal first-line tests confirm hypercortisolism
ACTH classifies dependent vs independent
Imaging follows biochemical confirmation
explanation.pearls: False positives on 1 mg DST occur with oestrogen (raised CBG), depression, alcohol use, obesity and pregnancy; consider these before pursuing further testing.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 58-year-old man in clinic 6 months after a first proximal lower limb DVT. He had no surgery, no immobility, no malignancy and no oestrogen use at the time. He has had a CT chest, abdomen and pelvis showing no malignancy, and is well. He has been on apixaban 5 mg twice daily. He has no bleeding history and tolerates apixaban well. He wants to know whether to continue, reduce or stop.

data_table.Examination: No leg swelling; no calf tenderness; cardiorespiratory examination normal
data_table.Investigations: Hb 142, plt 220, Cr 92, eGFR 84, INR 1.0, repeat lower limb Doppler shows residual non-occlusive thrombus; D-dimer 360 ng/mL (pending decision)
data_table.Medications: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily; nil other

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing anticoagulation plan?

options.text: Cease apixaban at 6 months; commence aspirin 100 mg daily for ongoing VTE prevention long-term
options.rationale: Aspirin offers some VTE prevention after stopping anticoagulation but is inferior to extended DOAC at standard or reduced dose for unprovoked VTE. (Source: ASTH VTE Position Statement)

options.text: Continue apixaban 5 mg twice daily indefinitely; review annually for bleeding risk and recurrence risk
options.rationale: Full-dose extended therapy is appropriate but the AMPLIFY-EXT trial supports reduced-dose apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily as the standard extended-phase dose, with similar efficacy and lower bleeding. (Source: ASTH VTE Position Statement)

options.text: Switch to warfarin with INR target 2.5 to 3.5 indefinitely; cease apixaban after 1 week of overlap with target INR
options.rationale: Higher-intensity warfarin is associated with more bleeding without efficacy benefit; DOACs are preferred in non-cancer non-valvular VTE. (Source: ASTH VTE Position Statement)

options.text: Reduce apixaban to 2.5 mg twice daily indefinitely; review annually for bleeding risk and recurrence risk
options.rationale: Unprovoked proximal DVT carries 10 percent annual recurrence risk; extended-phase apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily (AMPLIFY-EXT) is non-inferior to full dose with reduced bleeding and is the Australian standard. (Source: ASTH VTE Position Statement)

options.text: Cease apixaban at 6 months; arrange thrombophilia screen and IVC filter placement if any heritable thrombophilia is found
options.rationale: Routine thrombophilia screening rarely changes management; IVC filters are for contraindication to anticoagulation, not first-line VTE prevention. (Source: ASTH VTE Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Unprovoked proximal DVT or PE carries approximately 10 percent annual recurrence risk after stopping anticoagulation. Australian guidance recommends extended-phase anticoagulation beyond 3 to 6 months in patients without high bleeding risk. Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily (AMPLIFY-EXT) and rivaroxaban 10 mg daily (EINSTEIN-CHOICE) are non-inferior to full dose with lower bleeding. Provoked VTE with a transient risk factor stops at 3 months. Cancer-associated VTE continues while cancer is active. Bleeding risk is reviewed annually.
key_points: Unprovoked proximal VTE: extended anticoagulation beyond 6 months
Apixaban 2.5 mg BD or rivaroxaban 10 mg daily extended phase
Provoked VTE (transient): stop at 3 months
Cancer-associated VTE: continue while cancer active
explanation.pearls: AMPLIFY-EXT showed extended apixaban 2.5 mg BD reduced recurrence by 80 percent compared with placebo with bleeding rates similar to placebo.

subtopic_detail: Type B aortic dissection acute medical therapy
stem: You are the medical registrar in ED with a 62-year-old man with sudden severe tearing interscapular back pain 2 hours ago. CT aortogram confirms an uncomplicated Stanford type B aortic dissection extending from just distal to the left subclavian artery to the iliac bifurcation. There is no organ malperfusion, no rupture, and no rapid expansion. He has a known background of hypertension. He has no asthma.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 188/108 right arm and 184/106 left arm, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 98% RA, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Pain 8/10 between scapulae; equal radial pulses; warm peripheries; no neurological deficit; clear chest
data_table.Investigations: Troponin negative; Cr 96; CXR widened mediastinum; CT aortogram type B dissection without malperfusion
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 8 mg daily; amlodipine 10 mg daily at home

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial blood pressure and heart rate target and first-line therapy?

options.text: Sodium nitroprusside infusion alone titrated to systolic 100 to 120 mmHg; no heart rate target during titration
options.rationale: Vasodilator-only therapy causes reflex tachycardia and increased aortic wall shear stress; a beta-blocker must be started first or alongside to control heart rate. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Aortic Dissection Statement)

options.text: Intravenous metoprolol 5 mg bolus once; no further treatment if pain settles and discharge home from ED with GP follow-up
options.rationale: Acute aortic dissection requires admission to a critical care setting with continuous IV antihypertensive therapy, not single-bolus management and discharge. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Aortic Dissection Statement)

options.text: Urgent thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) within 4 hours; defer medical therapy until anaesthetic review for surgery
options.rationale: Uncomplicated type B dissection is managed medically first; TEVAR is for complicated dissection (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain, rapid expansion). (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Aortic Dissection Statement)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg with morphine 5 mg for pain; recheck BP at 30 minutes before further therapy
options.rationale: Frusemide does not address aortic wall shear; pain management is adjunctive; primary therapy is beta blockade plus vasodilator to lower systolic BP and dP/dt. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Aortic Dissection Statement)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol infusion targeting systolic 100 to 120 mmHg and heart rate below 60 within 20 minutes
options.rationale: Acute type B dissection requires immediate IV beta-blockade with systolic BP 100 to 120 mmHg and heart rate below 60 to reduce aortic wall shear stress and dP/dt; labetalol is the standard first agent in Australia. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Aortic Dissection Statement)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection requires immediate reduction of aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt) to limit propagation. First-line is intravenous beta blockade (labetalol or esmolol) targeting systolic BP 100 to 120 mmHg and heart rate below 60 per minute within 20 minutes. Vasodilators (sodium nitroprusside or GTN) are added after beta blockade if BP target not met. Pain control (IV opioid) reduces sympathetic drive. Uncomplicated type B is managed medically; type A and complicated type B require surgical or endovascular intervention.
key_points: Target SBP 100 to 120 mmHg and HR below 60 within 20 minutes
Beta blockade first; vasodilator second to avoid reflex tachycardia
Type A and complicated type B require surgery
Uncomplicated type B managed medically in ICU
explanation.pearls: Labetalol IV bolus 20 mg every 10 minutes (max 300 mg) or infusion at 0.5 to 2 mg/min is the Australian first-line agent; esmolol is an alternative when shorter half-life is preferred.
